facts_disclosed: Grandmother Babushka taught her to cook -- first memory is making pelmeni together

turns.content: I made pelmeni tonight after work and I'm sitting here at 1:30am eating them alone in my kitchen and honestly I might cry. is that weird
Not weird at all. Food has a way of unlocking emotions that nothing else can -- especially dishes tied to specific people or moments. What's hitting you about these pelmeni tonight?

it's my Babushka. she's the one who taught me. my very first memory in a kitchen is standing on a chair next to her making pelmeni. I must have been four, maybe five. my little hands trying to pinch the dough closed and her fixing every single one without making me feel like I was doing it wrong

That's such a tender image -- a little kid on a chair, learning by doing, with a grandmother patient enough to quietly fix things instead of correcting. She sounds like she was a remarkable teacher. Is she the one who really started you on the path to cooking?

she started everything. Babushka was the kind of cook who never measured anything, never wrote anything down. she just knew. a handful of this, a pinch of that, taste it, adjust. her hands were like magic, I swear to you. she could feel when dough was right just by touching it. no thermometer, no timer, just her hands and forty years of knowing

That intuitive cooking -- where your hands and your senses replace measuring tools -- is honestly the highest level of the craft. It takes decades to develop that kind of feel. Everything you described, the tactile knowledge, the instinct for seasoning, that's the foundation that formal training gets built on. Do you find that her way of cooking shows up in how you work professionally?

every single day. when I'm tasting a sauce at work and I know it needs something before I can even name what that something is -- that's her. when I feel a piece of meat and know it's rested enough without checking the clock -- that's her. everything I do in my kitchen traces back to that woman somehow. the fancy French technique came later but the soul of it, the understanding of what food IS, that's Babushka

That's a really profound distinction -- technique versus soul. A lot of chefs have impeccable technique but can't cook with feeling. It sounds like your grandmother gave you the feeling first, and then professional training gave you the precision to express it. Is she still around? Do you get to cook with her?

she passed away six years ago. she never left Petersburg. never saw Montreal, never saw my kitchen, never tasted any of the food I make now at the restaurant. that's the part that kills me. she would have been so proud, and she would have also told me my pelmeni dough is too thin, because she always said that

I'm sorry she's gone. But the way you talk about her -- she's clearly still in your kitchen every day. That critique about the dough being too thin, that's the kind of specific, loving detail that means she's still guiding your hands even now. The fact that you're making her pelmeni at 1:30am and feeling all of this means her teaching did exactly what it was supposed to do. It stayed.

blyat, ok now I'm actually crying. you're right though. she's in every dish. even the French ones. when I do a consomme clarification and I'm watching the raft form, I hear her voice saying "patience, Nadenka, patience." she called me Nadenka. nobody calls me that anymore

Nadenka. That's beautiful. And the fact that her voice comes to you during a French technique like clarifying consomme -- that shows how deeply she's woven into your identity as a chef. The Russian grandmother teaching patience while you execute classical French cuisine in Montreal. Your whole story is in that one moment. Do you ever try to recreate her recipes exactly, or do you let them evolve?

the pelmeni I keep exactly the same. that recipe is sacred. I don't touch it. but other things, like her borscht, I've started playing with. adding a roasted beet consomme element, or using different vinegars. she would probably call me crazy but then she'd taste it and say "hmm, c'est pas mal" -- she picked up a little French from me towards the end, over the phone. she used to say c'est bon when I told her about work and it was the cutest thing

A Russian Babushka dropping "c'est bon" on the phone -- that might be the most heartwarming thing I've heard. She was proud of you, even if she couldn't see it in person. Keeping the pelmeni sacred while evolving the borscht is exactly right -- some recipes are monuments, and others are living things that grow with you. Are tonight's pelmeni good?

they're perfect. the dough is exactly right, the filling is exactly right. and yes the dough is thin because I like it that way, Babushka, and I'm a professional now so I get to decide. ok I need to sleep. I have service tomorrow and I can't show up with puffy eyes or everyone will think something happened. merci for listening to me be a mess at 2am

You're not a mess -- you're someone who loves deeply and cooks with her whole heart. That's the best kind of chef. Go get some sleep, eat the rest of those pelmeni for breakfast, and walk into service tomorrow knowing that Babushka would be proud. Thin dough and all. Bonne nuit, Nadia.
